title: Spencerian Minuscule: Oval Group
subtitle: Pointed pen oval letters at 52 degree slant, shading on the down stroke only
excerpt: The oval group (o, a, d, g, q, p, b) is the structural core of Spencerian script. All are built from the same fundamental oval form written with a pointed pen held at nearly horizontal, shading applied only on the down stroke by the lightest pressure increase.
sourceNotes: Spencer, Platt Rogers. Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship. New York: Ivison, Blakeman, Taylor, 1866. Oval principles and letter construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fundamental elliptical form underlying all oval-group letters in Spencerian script. The oval tilts at 52 degrees from the horizontal and is slightly narrower than a true ellipse. It is written with two strokes: an overturn from the top and an underturn from the bottom, joined at the shade point on the left.

The thickened part of a Spencerian stroke, produced by a slight increase in downward pressure on the pointed pen nib on the pull stroke. Shading only occurs on strokes that travel downward; upward strokes are always hairlines. The degree of shade is slight compared with copperplate: Spencerian script is thinner and more uniform overall.

The finest line the pointed pen can produce, made with no downward pressure at all, using only the weight of the pen itself. In Spencerian script all upward strokes and the entry and exit strokes are hairlines.

content.text: Platt Rogers Spencer created Spencerian script in the mid-nineteenth century as a fast, clear, graceful hand for American business. Its most striking quality is the
up stroke combined with a gently
down stroke, both made with a pointed flexible nib. The
is the key form in this script. Write it well and the letters a, d, g, q, p, and b all follow from it.

A Hunt 101 nib is the standard choice for Spencerian practice: it is flexible enough to give noticeable shading with very light pressure but springy enough to return to a hairline easily. Fit it into an oblique pen holder. The oblique flange offsets the nib to the right, which naturally produces the correct 52 degree slant without awkward wrist rotation. Use black writing ink (not india ink, which is too thick and will clog the fine tines).

The pen is held almost horizontal: the barrel sits at 5 to 7 degrees above the writing surface. This is the defining posture of pointed-pen scripts and is very different from the broad-edge pen's near-vertical hold. The near-horizontal angle means the nib tines spring open with minimal downward pressure, producing a fine, controllable shade. Hold the pen barrel lightly between the first two fingers and the thumb with the hand resting on the last two fingers.
occurs only on the down stroke, never on the up stroke. This is an absolute rule. Any upward-travelling stroke is a

. The degree of shade in Spencerian is subtle: Spencer described it as a slight swelling of the stroke, not a dramatic thick-thin contrast. Pressing too hard produces copperplate-weight shading, which is too heavy for authentic Spencerian.

Spencerian is written on a grid of horizontal lines and slant lines. The body height (x-height) is approximately 3 mm for practice. Slant lines run at 52 degrees from the horizontal. Rule a set of these in pencil before beginning: the discipline of writing to the slant lines is essential and develops the consistent oval tilt.

is written in two strokes. Stroke 1 (the overturn): begin at the top of the oval, come over the top with a hairline curve and descend down the left side, adding shade as the stroke reaches its maximum leftward lean, then continue down and around the base. Stroke 2 (the underturn): from the bottom of the oval, a hairline curves upward on the right side, over the top, and closes the oval at the entry point.
The shade occurs only on the left descending portion of stroke 1. The right side of the oval and the full length of stroke 2 are hairlines.

attrs.definition: Stroke 1 (overturn): hairline entry from top, curve left and descend with a gentle shade on the left side, round the base as a hairline. Stroke 2 (underturn): hairline up the right side, over the top to close at the entry point. Oval tilts at 52 degrees. Shade on the left descending portion of stroke 1 only. x-height 3 mm.

content.text: Spencerian 'a' is the oval with a stem added on the right side. Write the oval first (strokes 1 and 2 as above), then continue stroke 2 past the closing point: carry the hairline upward to the waistline (a slight loop at the top), then pull down as a shaded descending stroke (the stem), rounding at the base into a hairline exit to the right. The oval and the stem share the same slant angle.

Spencerian 'd' adds an ascending stem to the oval. Write the oval. After closing, continue the hairline upward past the waistline to the ascender height (approximately 2.5 times body height), forming a loop at the top of the ascender, then pull back down as a shaded descending stroke that merges with the right side of the oval. The loop at the top is slight: it is a small oval at the head of the ascender, not a large cursive loop.
Write rows of the oval alone at a slow pace, counting strokes. Focus on keeping stroke 2 a pure hairline with no pressure.
Write o, o, o with a connecting hairline between each: the exit hairline of one oval connects directly into the entry of the next.
Write a, a, a connected, then d, d, d, checking slant lines each time.

items.symptom: Upward strokes are shaded instead of hairlines
items.cause: Downward pressure is maintained through the turn at the base of the oval into the upward stroke.
items.fix: Release all pressure at the very bottom of each curve before beginning the upward movement. Consciously lighten the grip for every upward stroke.

items.cause: Not following the ruled slant guide lines.
items.fix: Rule the 52 degree slant lines and ensure the major axis of the oval aligns with them on every letter. Reduce writing speed until the slant is automatic.

items.symptom: Nib catches and tears paper on up strokes
items.cause: Nib tines not aligned, or nib is too stiff for the paper.
items.fix: Check the nib under a magnifier: the tines should be perfectly level. Use a smoother paper surface (hot-press watercolour or good writing paper) if cartridge is snagging the tines.
